title: Sagittarius A* — Milky Way Galactic Center Black Hole

shots.speech_text: Imagine 4.297 million. That single number is the key to understanding Sagittarius A* — Milky Way Galactic Center Black Hole — one of the most dramatic and ancient phenomena in the known universe.
shots.visual_direction: OPEN ON: [High-impact visual — The EHT image of Sgr A* shows a glowing orange-gold ring of emission — similar in visual character to M87* but notably f]. Title card: 'Sagittarius A* — Milky Way Galactic Center Black Hole'. No host. Pure visual with HELIX voiceover.

shots.speech_text: Sagittarius A* — Milky Way Galactic Center Black Hole sits within the domain of astronomy. General relativity predicts that the photon capture cross-section of a black hole forms an observable 'shadow' — for Sgr A*, this region subtends ~52 microarcseconds, making it the largest apparent angular size of any black hole as seen from Earth despite its relatively modest mass. To understand it, we need to think at a scale that challenges intuition.
shots.visual_direction: ESTABLISH SHOT: [Wide or macro view establishing scale and context of Sagittarius A* — Milky Way Galactic Center Black Hole]. Slow zoom or pull-back revealing scope. Minimal text — let the imagery speak.
shots.vo_notes: HELIX voiceover — warm measured female voice, slight British RP accent, inviting and professional — a trusted guide through the unknown, never clinical, wonder without hyperbole
shots.role_description: Set the scene: what is this phenomenon, where does it occur, what scale are we at

shots.speech_text: General relativity predicts that the photon capture cross-section of a black hole forms an observable 'shadow' — for Sgr A*, this region subtends ~52 microarcseconds, making it the largest apparent angular size of any black hole as seen from Earth despite its relatively modest mass Stellar orbital tracking of the S-star cluster — particularly the star S2, which completes a full orbit in 16 years — provides an independent precise measurement of Sgr A*'s mass and the Earth-to-galactic-center distance The governing framework here is General relativity; Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI); Keplerian orbital mechanics of S-stars; Near-infrared interferometry (GRAVITY/VLTI).
shots.visual_direction: EXPLAIN SEQUENCE: [Animation or diagram illustrating the core mechanism of Sagittarius A* — Milky Way Galactic Center Black Hole]. Clear, elegant — no clutter. Labels only where essential. Motion should mirror the narration pace.
shots.vo_notes: HELIX voiceover — warm measured female voice, slight British RP accent, inviting and professional — a trusted guide through the unknown, never clinical, wonder without hyperbole
shots.role_description: Core mechanism or concept — clearly, accurately, engagingly. No jargon without definition

shots.speech_text: What you're seeing here — The EHT image of Sgr A* shows a glowing orange-gold ring of emission — similar in visual character to M87* but notably fuzzier in the released image due to rapid variability during.
shots.visual_direction: VISUALIZATION: [The EHT image of Sgr A* shows a glowing orange-gold ring of emission — similar in visual character to M87* but notably fuzzier in the released image due to rapid variability during the observation. The ring is roughly symmetric (unlike M87*'s asymmetric crescent), consistent with a nearly face-on accretion disk orientation. Shadow diameter: ~52 microarcseconds — coincidentally almost identical apparent size to M87* in the sky, despite Sgr A* being ~1,500x less massive and ~2,000x closer. The physical shadow diameter is ~51.8 million km (~0.35 AU), smaller than Mercury's orbit. The Schwarzschild radius is 0.08 AU (12 million km). For S-star visualization: the S2 star traces a tight Keplerian ellipse around an invisible point (the black hole) with a 16-year period — a powerful 'proving' visual. The galactic center is hidden behind ~25 magnitudes of interstellar dust extinction in visible light — only radio, infrared, and X-ray can see Sgr A*. Shot ideas: galaxy overhead view showing Earth-to-center distance of 26,000 light-years; S2 orbital animation; zoom to 52-microarcsecond shadow ring; comparison frame with M87* at same angular scale.]. HELIX narrates over continuous visual — no cuts interrupting the flow.
shots.vo_notes: HELIX voiceover — warm measured female voice, slight British RP accent, inviting and professional — a trusted guide through the unknown, never clinical, wonder without hyperbole
shots.role_description: Describe what the camera or animation shows — specific, visual, sensory language

shots.speech_text: The numbers confirm it. EHT released first image of Sagittarius A* on May 12, 2022, showing a ring-like structure with angular size 51.8 ± 2.3 microarcseconds consistent with the predicted black hole shadow. This result — published by Event Horizon Telescope Collaboration 2022, ApJL 930 L12 (Paper I: Introduction and Summary) — represents one of the landmark measurements in modern astronomy.
shots.visual_direction: EVIDENCE SHOT: [Data visualisation, telescope imagery, or archival footage of the key discovery moment]. Institution logos/credits if available. Measurement values as lower-third text overlay.
shots.vo_notes: HELIX voiceover — warm measured female voice, slight British RP accent, inviting and professional — a trusted guide through the unknown, never clinical, wonder without hyperbole
shots.role_description: The key measurement, landmark discovery, or institutional proof — cite it precisely

shots.speech_text: Sagittarius A* — Milky Way Galactic Center Black Hole is not just a phenomenon at the far edge of science. It connects directly to questions about our own origins. The Stellar orbital tracking of the S-star cluster — particularly the star S2, which completes a full orbit in 16 years — provides an independent precise measurement of Sgr A*'s mass and the Earth-to-galactic-center distance has implications that reach far beyond the laboratory.
shots.visual_direction: CONNECT SHOT: [Imagery bridging Sagittarius A* — Milky Way Galactic Center Black Hole to human scale or everyday context — e.g. night sky, biological cell, chemical process]. Gentle visual metaphor. Warmer colour grade than previous shots.

shots.speech_text: We are only beginning to understand Sagittarius A* — Milky Way Galactic Center Black Hole. And perhaps that's exactly the point — the universe keeps its most ancient secrets just within reach, if we keep looking.
shots.visual_direction: CLOSE: [Wide, contemplative — return to establishing imagery or slow fade to black]. HELIX narration ends. Silence before credits. No host sign-off.

provenance_card.title: Sagittarius A* — Milky Way Galactic Center Black Hole
provenance_card.key_measurement: Mass: 4.297 ± 0.012 × 10⁶ solar masses (4.297 million M☉); Distance: 26,996 ± 33 light-years (8,277 ± 9 parsecs); Shadow ring angular size: 51.8 ± 2.3 microarcseconds (EHT Collaboration 2022, ApJL 930 L12; GRAVITY Collaboration 2022)
provenance_card.framework: General relativity; Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI); Keplerian orbital mechanics of S-stars; Near-infrared interferometry (GRAVITY/VLTI)
institutions: Max Planck Institute for Extraterrestrial Physics (MPE) — Reinhard Genzel group
UCLA Galactic Center Group — Andrea Ghez group
GRAVITY Collaboration / ESO Very Large Telescope Interferometer

sources.title: EHT released first image of Sagittarius A* on May 12, 2022, showing a ring-like structure with angular size 51.8 ± 2.3 microarcseconds consistent with the predicted black hole shadow
sources.citation: Event Horizon Telescope Collaboration 2022, ApJL 930 L12 (Paper I: Introduction and Summary)

sources.title: Mass of Sgr A* measured at 4.297 ± 0.012 × 10⁶ solar masses and distance 8,277 ± 9 pc (26,996 ly) from GRAVITY interferometer tracking S2 stellar orbit at the VLT
Sgr A* discovered as a compact radio source in 1974 by Balick and Brown using NRAO baseline interferometer; the asterisk designation added 1982 by Brown to denote 'excited' nature of the source
Reinhard Genzel and Andrea Ghez awarded the 2020 Nobel Prize in Physics for discovering that Sgr A* is a supermassive compact object — the best empirical evidence that supermassive black holes exist
sources.citation: Nobel Committee for Physics 2020, Nobel Prize in Physics announcement

sources.title: Star S2 reached closest approach (periapsis) to Sgr A* in 2018 at ~120 AU, moving at 2.55% the speed of light (7,650 km/s), with gravitational redshift observed consistent with general relativity

provenance_card.accuracy_note: All measurements cited. Uncertainty stated where present. No fabricated statistics.

guardrails: HELIX is voiceover only — no on-screen host, no synthetic presenter, no avatar.
All quantitative claims must include units and citation.
Uncertainty must be stated where present (e.g. 'approximately', 'within a margin of').
Do not present theoretical models as confirmed fact without qualification.
Do not fabricate measurements, paper titles, DOIs, or institutional affiliations.
Maintain wonder without hyperbole — the phenomena are extraordinary enough.
No speculation presented as established science.
HELIX does not cover history, biography, or language — those are DAVID's domain.
